ideas.intro: Ideas for projects, activities, books, games, and more for students with a high level of interest.

items.content:
[ ] Go fishing.
[ ] Play the "Slippery Stick Game."
[ ] Make a totem pole out of an empty paper towel roll. Can you use it to tell the story of your family?
[ ] Visit a museum or learning center for people of the Pacific Northwest.

[ ] Visit a museum or learning center for people of the Pacific Northwest.
[ ] Visit a desert or a prairie if you live near enough.
[ ] Make a moonstick and mark each full moon. (You can find the dates [here](https://www.astronomy.com/observing/full-moon-calendar-dates-times-types-2024/).) Use the traditional names given, or make up your own according to what you notice happening in nature where you live.

[ ] Try hominy.
[ ] Visit a museum or learning center for people of the Great Lakes or the Eastern Woodlands.
[ ] Find what [tribal nations](https://native-land.ca/) lived in your area and learn more about them.

resources.title: Buffalo Woman
resources.author: Paul Goble
resources.rationale: A young hunter draws his bow against a buffalo cow drinking from a stream. Before he can loose his arrow, there is no buffalo -- instead, there stands a beautiful young woman, whom he knows he must marry.

resources.title: Clamshell Boy: A Makah Legend
resources.author: Terri Cohlene
resources.rationale: This book retells the legend of Clamshell Boy, who rescues a captured group of children from the dreaded wild woman Ishcus; includes information on the customs and lifestyle of the Makah Indians.

resources.title: Her Seven Brothers
resources.author: Paul Goble
resources.rationale: When an Indian girl begins to make clothes beautifully decorated with porcupine quills for seven brothers she has not yet met, her parents believe that unseen powers have spoken to her.

resources.title: Hiawatha
resources.author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow; illus. Susan Jeffers
resources.rationale: This episode of the classic poem tells about the child Hiawatha and his mother.

resources.title: How the Stars Fell Into the Sky: A Navajo Legend
resources.author: Jerrie Oughton
resources.rationale: This retelling of a Navajo folktale explains how First Woman tried to write the laws of the land using stars in the sky, only to be thwarted by the trickster Coyote.

resources.title: Ka-ha-si and the Loon: An Eskimo Legend
resources.author: Terri Cohlene
resources.rationale: In this beloved Eskimo legend, an idle sleepyhead becomes the champion who saves his people from famine and disaster.

resources.title: North American Indian
resources.author: David Murdoch
resources.rationale: Children will enjoy looking at pictures of Native American artifacts as they read about the many diverse tribes.

resources.title: Quillworker: A Cheyenne Legend
resources.author: Terri Cohlene
resources.rationale: This legend tells about the origin of stars.

resources.title: Raven: A Trickster Tale From the Pacific Northwest
resources.author: Gerald McDermott
resources.rationale: Raven, the trickster, wants to give people the gift of light. But can he find out where Sky Chief keeps it? And if he does, will he be able to escape without being discovered? His dream seems impossible, but if anyone can find a way to bring light to the world, wise and clever Raven can!

resources.title: Sootface: An Ojibwa Cinderella Story
resources.author: Robert D. San Souci
resources.rationale: Many cultures, including Native Americans, have Cinderella stories. See how this one compares with the one you know.

resources.title: Tales the Totems Tell
resources.author: Hugh Weatherby
resources.rationale: This excerpt contains the story of the first totem pole and the most common totem figure, the raven.

resources.title: The Legend of Hobbomock: The Sleeping Giant
resources.author: Jason J. Marchi
resources.rationale: This is the Quinnipiac tribal legend of how the Sleeping Giant landform in Hamden, Connecticut came to look like a giant man sleeping on his back.

resources.title: The Legend of the Bluebonnet: An Old Tale of Texas
resources.author: Tomie dePaola
resources.rationale: When a killing drought threatens the existence of the tribe, a courageous Comanche girl sacrifices her most beloved possession--and the Great Spirit's answer results not only in much-needed rain but a special gift in return.

resources.title: The Legend of the Indian Paintbrush
resources.author: Tomie dePaola
resources.rationale: In spring, the hills and meadows of Texas and Wyoming are ablaze with the reds, oranges, and yellows of the Indian Paintbrush. How this striking plant received its name is told in an old Indian legend.

resources.title: The Rough-Face Girl
resources.author: Rafe Martin
resources.rationale: Compare this Algonquin Cinderella story to the Ojibwa story.

resources.title: They Dance in the Sky: Native American Star Myths
resources.author: Jean Guard Monroe & Ray A. Williamson
resources.rationale: This book tells the Native American stories behind constellations. It would be particularly good for those with high interest and some background in astronomy.

resources.title: Thirteen Moons on Turtle's Back: A Native American Year of Moons
resources.author: Joseph Bruchac & Jonathan London
resources.rationale: In Native American legend, the thirteen scales on a turtle's back symbolize the annual thirteen cycles of the moon and the seasonal changes each one brings. The thirteen poems in this book -- one for each moon of the year, and each from a different indigenous group -- celebrate the natural world and weave together traditional knowledge and folklore. Each poem is illustrated by a beautiful painting.

resources.title: Wigwam Stories Told by North American Indians
resources.author: Mary Catherine Judd
resources.rationale: This is a collection of traditional stories from various indigenous tribes. This book is in the Public Domain and can be accessed on Hathitrust.org.
